text.fullText: February 17, 2026 Ms. Lauren Kasparek U.S. Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW Washington, DC 20460 Docket No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 [SUBMITTED ELECTRONICALLY] RE: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations The Edison Electric Institute (EEI) appreciates the opportunity to submit comments on the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA or Agency) proposed rule, Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (Proposed Rule). 91 Fed. Reg. 2,008 (January 15, 2026). EEI generally supports the Proposed Rule, and in these comments offers modifications to further improve the certification process and facilitate the continued buildout of infrastructure required to meet American energy needs. EEI is the leading voice and advocate for investor-owned electric companies in the United States, which serve more than 250 million customers in 50 states and the District of Columbia. EEI member companies work every day to provide reliable energy to their customers at the lowest cost possible. EEI member companies also form the backbone of our nation’s electric power industry, which is essential to advancing our economic and national security priorities, including achieving global dominance in artificial intelligence (AI) and advanced computing technologies. Over the next five years, investor-owned electric companies will invest more than $1.1 trillion to enhance the electric grid, building critically needed new generation and transmission and distribution infrastructure required to power economic prosperity. EEI members own, operate, build, and maintain electric power generation, transmission, and distribution facilities requiring federal licenses or permits that deliver electric power throughout the U.S. These activities may result in discharges into navigable waters. EEI members are therefore directly impacted by the CWA Section 401 certification process and have a significant interest in its implementation. An efficient, consistent, and predictable certification process that simultaneously protects water quality is critical to EEI members’ goals of continuing to provide reliable energy at the lowest cost. Questions on these comments may be directed to Patrick McGuire (202-508-5167) or Daniel Whittle (202-508-5990). Sincerely, M. Patrick McGuire Associate General Counsel COMMENTS FROM THE EDISON ELECTRIC INSTITUTE ON THE U.S. ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION AGENCY’S PROPOSED RULE, UPDATING WATER QUALITY CERTIFICATION REGULATIONS Docket No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 February 17, 2026 The Edison Electric Institute (EEI) appreciates the opportunity to submit comments on the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA or Agency) proposed rule, Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (Proposed Rule). 91 Fed. Reg. 2,008 (January 15, 2026). Through the Proposed Rule, EPA plans to update and clarify several substantive and procedural requirements for water quality certification under Clean Water Act (CWA or the Act) Section 401. EEI generally supports the Proposed Rule, and in these comments offers modifications to further improve the certification process and facilitate the continued buildout of infrastructure required to meet American energy needs. EEI is the leading voice and advocate for investor-owned electric companies in the United States, which serve more than 250 million customers in 50 states and the District of Columbia. EEI member companies work every day to provide reliable energy to their customers at the lowest cost possible. EEI member companies also form the backbone of our nation’s electric power industry, which is essential to advancing our economic and national security priorities, including achieving global dominance in artificial intelligence (AI) and advanced computing technologies. Over the next five years, investor-owned electric companies will invest more than $1.1 trillion to enhance the electric grid, building critically needed new generation and transmission and distribution infrastructure required to power economic prosperity. 1 CWA Section 401 grants states and Tribes that have been approved for “treatment as a state” status the authority to review activities requiring a federal license or permit that may result in a discharge into “navigable waters” to certify that any such discharge will comply with certain applicable federal, state, and tribal water quality requirements. See 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a). CWA Section 401 thus carves out an important water quality protection role for states and authorized Tribes in federal licensing and permitting processes. EEI members own, operate, build, and maintain electric power generation, transmission, and distribution facilities requiring federal licenses or permits that deliver electric power throughout the U.S. These activities may result in discharges into navigable waters. EEI members are therefore directly impacted by the CWA Section 401 certification process and have a significant interest in its implementation. An efficient, consistent, and predictable certification process that simultaneously protects water quality is critical to EEI members’ goals of continuing to provide reliable energy at the lowest cost. I. Electric Companies Are Investing to Meet Unprecedented Demand Growth. Across the country, investor-owned electric companies are investing in a broad range of technologies to deliver reliable power at the lowest reasonable cost to meet customers’ needs while also satisfying federal and state requirements. Though EEI member companies operate in differing regulatory environments and face varied customer demands, we share a clear message: to meet our nation’s needs in this moment, we must use every available tool to deploy new generation capacity and maximize the capabilities of our transmission and distribution infrastructure. 2 As EEI members drive to serve our customers at the lowest cost, customer needs are rapidly growing. The power sector is facing unprecedented levels of demand growth. Projections consistently show a significant amount of new generation capacity must come online in the years ahead, mostly due to increasing manufacturing and the rapid proliferation of AI and advanced computing technologies. This represents both a tremendous opportunity and a challenge for the power sector, after nearly two decades of minimal increases in net electricity demand and in the face of high development costs, supply chain and workforce pressures, affordability concerns for customers, and siting and permitting barriers. EEI members are well-positioned to meet this challenge and support this national priority, with plans for $1.1 trillion in grid investments during the next five years to better serve our customers and facilitate data center and major industrial development, while also achieving reliability and securing broader benefits for existing customers and communities. II. EEI Supports Finalization of EPA’s Clarification Regarding the Scope of Certification. EPA proposes to clarify that “[t]he scope of certification is limited to assuring that a discharge from a federally licensed or permitted activity will comply with applicable and appropriate water quality requirements.” 91 Fed. Reg. at 2,040 (to be codified at 40 C.F.R. § 121.3). Further, EPA proposed to define “discharge” as a “discharge from a point source into waters of the United States.” Id. (to be codified at 40 C.F.R. § 121.1). EEI supports finalizing this clarification. CWA Section 401(a)(1) states that the state licensing or permitting agency must certify that “any such discharge will comply with” applicable provisions of the CWA for any applicant for a federal license or permit to conduct any activity that “may result in any discharge into the navigable waters.” See 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1). This section refers to the 3 importance of the discharge itself for a certification multiple times. Therefore, EPA’s clarification aligns the regulatory text with both the language and the intent of the CWA. III. EEI Supports Clarification of the One-Year Certification Timeframe. CWA Section 401 states that the certifying authority must act on a request for certification “within a reasonable period of time (which shall not exceed one year).” See 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1). EEI members have experienced complications in multiple states regarding the one- year timeframe and therefore EEI supports EPA clarifying this timeframe in the Proposed Rule. Specifically, EEI supports EPA’s proposal to clarify that the timeline starts when the application is submitted; that resubmittal of additional information does not restart the timeline; and, that failure by the certifying authority to act on a request within the reasonable period of time results in a waiver. See 91 Fed. Reg. at 2,041. These clarifications provide certainty and predictability for EEI members throughout the country seeking certifications. EEI also supports EPA’s clarification that a certifying authority may not request withdrawal and resubmittal of a request for certification in order to extend the reasonable period of time. Id. (to be codified at 40 C.F.R. § 121.6(e)). EEI members have experienced the negative consequences of this approach in the past and support EPA providing clarification that authorities may no longer delay certification in this way. IV. EEI Supports the Uniformity and Certainty in the Proposed Rule Regarding the Required Elements of a Certification Request. EPA proposes to specify an enumerated list of documents and information that must be included in a request for certification for all Federal licenses or permits, and to remove regulatory text allowing states and tribes to require additional types of information in a request for certification. See 90 Fed. Reg. at 2,017 (to be codified at 40 C.F.R. § 121.5). EEI supports EPA providing uniformity and certainty across the country regarding the required elements of a 4 certification request. The certainty and predictability of a uniform request for certification will promote more efficient application processes for EEI members that operate across multiple states and tribal jurisdictions. It will allow members to file one unified application instead of multiple separate certification applications with different requirements across several jurisdictions, thereby streamlining the application process and speeding certification for projects that cross wide geographical ranges. V. EEI Supports EPA Clearly Defining a Role for Project Proponents Regarding Modifications of Certification Conditions. EPA proposes requiring the Federal agency, the certifying authority, and the applicant to all agree in writing that the certifying authority may modify a grant of certification (with or without conditions), before any modification occurs. See 90 Fed. Reg. at 2,041 (to be codified at 40 C.F.R. § 121.10). EEI supports this change for the same reason as the other proposed changes: it will provide certainty and predictability to applicants. In the past, EEI members have experienced circumstances where a certifying authority unilaterally pushes for impractical or unnecessary modifications to certification conditions. Requiring all parties to agree on modification will help ensure the modification is necessary and will lead to the best possible outcome for the application. VI. EPA Should Consider Exempting Facilities from CWA Section 401 Certification if the Facility already has a National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System Permit for the Relevant Discharges. In some instances, states have required CWA Section 401 certification where the facility in question has already established compliance with water quality requirements through an existing valid National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (NPDES) permit issued pursuant to 5 Section 402 of the CWA by a state under delegated authority from EPA. 1 To the extent that a 401 certification is required for discharges to navigable waters or other applicable requirements included in the NPDES permitting process, EPA should clarify that a valid NPDES permit indicates compliance with water quality requirements under CWA Section 401. Indeed, a NPDES permit cannot be issued “when the imposition of conditions cannot ensure compliance with the applicable water quality requirements of all affected States,” or, for a new source, “if the discharge from its construction or operation will cause or contribute to the violation of water quality standards.” 2 This clarification will remove duplicative processes and therefore streamline compliance with the CWA. We emphasize that the limited role of Section 401 is demonstrated by the history of the provision as compared to that of Section 402, which created the CWA’s discharge permitting process. Prior to the passage of the modern CWA in 1972, Section 21(b) of the Water Quality Improvement Act contained a certification provision identical to Section 401, which applied to projects that did not proceed through a federally enforceable discharge permitting process, as that process did not yet exist. The Water Quality Improvement Act’s certification provision thus predated the development of the more comprehensive CWA permitting process under Section 402. Congress’ transference of the prior certification provision into Section 401 of the new CWA 1 These comments use “NPDES” to refer to CWA Section 402 permits issued by state authorities operating permitting programs under EPA delegated authority. Some states call these “SPDES” programs, the “S” standing for “State”, or insert the name of the state (“Texas Pollution Discharge Elimination System” or TPDES). These are state-issued permits that do not require a 401 certification. It is important to note that a CWA Section 402 permit issued by EPA itself, as for a non-delegated state, tribe, or territory, is a federally issued permit that must be supported by a 401 certification. 40 C.F.R. § 124.53(a). 2 40 C.F.R. § 122.4(d), (i). 6 in 1972, along with the creation of the NPDES process in Section 402, created two processes that should be read as not duplicative. Section 401 becomes duplicative, contrary to this statutory structure, when it is applied to projects that also must undergo Section 402 permitting. Congress’ inclusion of both Section 401 and the new Section 402 in the modern CWA creates a simple coordinated process. At its core, Section 401 is a pre-construction approval process, 3 whereas Section 402 permits are required for operations, not for construction. Section 401 also logically exists to apply pre-construction review to those projects to which the new Section 402 did not apply, just as it did prior to 1972 before the NPDES process existed— allowing the state to condition projects that otherwise will not undergo permitting. It is only rational that states would look to the Section 402 permitting process for continuing protection of water quality for projects whose operations result in a discharge. Therefore, based on the appropriate limited role of Section 401 in the overall CWA system, and given the requirement that, at least where discharges are concerned, NPDES permits must protect water quality standards, EPA should provide guidance to the states that: (1) the existence of a valid Section 402 permit provides reasonable assurance that the discharge will comply with water quality requirements, and (2) that this reasonable assurance creates a basis on which a 401 certification can be issued or, if the state prefers, waived. VII. EPA Should Revise the Pre-Filing Meeting Request Requirement. The Proposed Rule requires project proponents to request a pre-filing meeting from the certifying authority at least 30 days prior to a certification request submission. However, certifying authorities are allowed to waive or shorten this requirement. See 40 C.F.R. § 121.4. 3 See, e.g., § 401(a)(3), which explains that absent changed circumstances, permittees need not obtain a certification for operation when one has been obtained pre-construction. 7 In the experience of EEI members, the pre-filing meeting request requirement delays small projects that have minimal to no water quality impact. EEI members have noted that after a certifying authority receives a pre-filing meeting request for minor projects with minimal or no water quality impact, the certifying authority will then often wait the full 30 days before proceeding with the certification even if there are no other changes. As a result, the pre-filing requirement has become a de facto 30-day delay for small, short-lived, and generally non- controversial construction or maintenance projects. This type of delay is unnecessary, and the pre-filing meeting request process ends up slowing down certification with no concomitant benefit to the environment. EPA should instead make the pre-filing meeting request voluntary— or consider creating categorical exemptions from the requirement for smaller, less complex projects that have minimal or no water quality impacts, or need to be done on an emergency basis. At a minimum, EPA should consider allowing project proponents to participate in the certifying authority’s waiver process for determining whether the pre-filing meeting is necessary. These changes would provide flexibility and facilitate quicker certifications for the critical infrastructure projects EEI members conduct that have minimal or no water quality impacts. VIII. EPA Should Assert in Regulatory Text that Withdrawals from Navigable Waters Do Not Trigger the CWA Section 401 Process. Courts have concluded that a water withdrawal is not a discharge and therefore does not trigger the need for a water quality certification. 4 EPA appropriately clarified in the preamble to the 2023 rule that withdrawals from navigable waters neither trigger the CWA Section 401 4 See, e.g., North Carolina v. FERC, 112 F.3d 1175, 1187 (D.C. Cir. 1997) (holding that withdrawal of water from lake does not constitute discharge for CWA Section 401 purposes). 8 process, nor are they “discharges” whose impacts may be addressed by Section 401 certification conditions. 87 Fed. Reg. at 35,329. 5 Further, EPA appears to assert withdrawals from navigable waters do not trigger the CWA Section 401 process in the Proposed Rule by narrowing the scope of the certifying authority review to just an assessment of whether a facility’s point source discharges into waters of the United States comply with applicable water quality requirements, but does not explicitly exempt withdrawals from the certification process. 91 Fed. Reg. at 2,023. To provide clarity, the Agency should explicitly exempt withdrawals in the regulatory text. IX. Conclusion. EEI supports EPA’s finalization of the Proposed Rule with the modifications explained above. Questions on these comments may be directed to Patrick McGuire (202-508-5167) or Daniel Whittle (202-508-5990). 5 See also EPA, 2023 Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule Final 2023 Rule Questions & Answers (last updated Nov. 2024) at 6, https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2024-12/qa-for-2023-cwa-section-401-rule- implementation-112024.pdf. 9
